# What is a serial interval?

## Two delays: serial and generation interval

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="40%"}
- **Serial interval (SI)**: time between *symptom onset* of infector
  and infectee
- **Generation interval (GI)**: time between *infections*
- The SI can be **negative**: the infectee's symptoms can appear
  before the infector's, for example with presymptomatic transmission
- The GI is what *some* transmission models need; the SI is usually
  what we get to observe
:::
::: {.column width="60%"}
![](figures/fig-gt-vs-si-timeline.png){width="100%"}

::: {.attribution}
Source: within-host informed event-time framework (Jamieson et al.,
working paper)
:::
:::
::::

## But we rarely observe this exactly

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="40%"}
- Infection and transmission times are **latent** — we almost never
  see them directly
- Symptom onsets are recorded to a **day** at best, and "onset" is
  itself imprecise: definitions vary, and recall is biased
- So we never fully observe this picture: the events we want are
  **interval censored**, and in real time **right truncated**
- Estimating a serial interval is really estimating a delay from
  incomplete data
:::
::: {.column width="60%"}
![](figures/fig-gt-vs-si-timeline-observed.png){width="100%"}

::: {.attribution}
Source: within-host informed event-time framework (Jamieson et al.,
working paper)
:::
:::
::::

## Why do we want it?

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="52%"}
- Feeds $R_t$ through the renewal equation, and links the growth
  rate $r$ to the reproduction number $R$
- Beyond renewal: used to **reconstruct infection times** from
  onsets — the Andes-virus work below back-calculates who infected
  whom and when
- Also feeds forecasting and contact tracing
- But the SI is a **proxy** for the generation interval, the quantity
  some transmission models need
:::
::: {.column width="48%"}
::: {.callout-note}
Defined from the right reference cohort, the forward SI links $r$ to
$R$ just as the GI does.
:::

::: {.attribution}
[Park et al. (2021)](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.2011548118);
[Lehtinen et al. (2021)](https://doi.org/10.1098/rsif.2020.0756);
[Wallinga & Lipsitch (2007)](https://doi.org/10.1098/rspb.2006.3754)
:::
:::
::::

## How do we currently suggest people estimate and report a delay distribution?

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
- Adjust for **within-interval censoring of both events** (always)
- **Right truncation** is the correction for dynamical
  (epidemic-phase) bias
- Fit **several distributions** and compare with model-fit statistics
- Report uncertainty, and share de-identified line lists and code

::: {.callout-note}
The Andes-virus analysis below works through a version of this
checklist:
[andv-linelist-analysis](https://epiforecasts.io/andv-linelist-analysis/dev/charniga).
:::

::: {.attribution}
Flowchart: Fig 3 of
[Charniga et al. (2024)](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pcbi.1012520),
PLOS Comput Biol, CC-BY 4.0
:::
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
![](figures/fig-charniga-flowchart.png){height="840px"}
:::
::::
